Not Financial Advice! Just research!

@SpaceX is preparing for an IPO with $2 Trillion valuation, so market participants do see a massive business here in space, especially AI in Space or Data Center in Space and much more. 

I know I know what you gonna say, Mike already talking about future revenue like 2022/2023(OpenAi,Meta, Oracle, MSFT, AWS...). That is why you have to understand Dr. Su, she engineers AMD to be 3-5 years ahead in diversified product-led strategy. It is not luck that we got here today.

AMD (through its 2022 acquisition of Xilinx) holds a strong 55% market share, frequently leading position in radiation-tolerant and radiation-hardened FPGAs and adaptive SoCs for space applications. This is especially true for high-performance, reconfigurable computing and onboard AI inference in satellites, rovers, and lunar/deep-space missions. While the overall "AI in space" hardware segment remains a small, specialized niche within the broader radiation-hardened electronics market, AMD/Xilinx benefits from decades of flight heritage and expanding commercial partnerships. Exact market share percentages for the ultra-niche AI-onboard segment are not publicly disclosed due to its fragmented, long-cycle nature, but AMD is widely recognized as a top player in reconfigurable space-grade solutions. 

"AI in space" encompassing onboard autonomy, real-time inference, anomaly detection, data reduction, and signal processing,  forms a faster growing but still tiny subset within the hundreds of millions today. Broader AI in space exploration/operations is estimated at $135 billion in 2026, with aggressive projections reaching USD $300+ billion by 2030 or much higher if Data Center in space gets the capital injection. Dedicated onboard AI hardware (accelerators and adaptive computing for edge/space constraints) represents only a fraction of this, driven by exploding LEO mega-constellations, NASA's Artemis program, Mars exploration, and the push for spacecraft independence to handle massive data volumes while minimizing downlink needs. Space's extreme constraints (radiation, power/thermal limits, vacuum, and multi-year missions) favor adaptive SoCs over commercial GPUs or traditional rad-hard CPUs.

1. AMD/Xilinx's Technological Edge and Flight Heritage

Xilinx (now @AMD ) pioneered SRAM-based, reprogrammable FPGAs for space, with families like Virtex-5QV, Kintex UltraScale , and now the Versal adaptive SoC portfolio (including space-grade/rad-tolerant AI Core and AI Edge variants, such as Gen 2). These devices integrate programmable logic (FPGA fabric), dedicated AI Engines for efficient inference, Arm processors, and built-in radiation mitigation for single-event upsets, total ionizing dose (TID), and latch-up immunity, with Class B and Class Y qualifications for missions up to 15 years.

Heritage includes NASA's Mars rovers (Perseverance for image processing and navigation), OSIRIS-REx, Artemis II (supporting avionics and sensor systems on SLS rocket and Orion spacecraft), and the NISAR Earth observation satellite. AMD continues to expand its space-grade Versal lineup for enhanced in-orbit processing

2. Key partners and commercial wins

A. SpaceX (Starlink): A major win. AMD's Versal AI Core adaptive SoCs power the latest-generation Starlink broadband satellites (V2 Mini and subsequent variants). This was highlighted by AMD CEO Dr. Lisa Su, enabling high-performance onboard processing, signal handling, networking, and potential AI tasks in the massive LEO constellation.

B. Blue Origin: Integrating Versal AI Edge Gen 2 adaptive SoCs into development flight computers for the Mark 2 lunar lander (targeting crewed landings as early as 2028). These support signal processing, deterministic control, and onboard AI under strict SWaP-C constraints.

C. NEC (Japan): Deploying Versal adaptive SoCs in Japan's first optical communication satellite constellation for high-speed network routing and signal processing.

D. NASA and Artemis Program: Long-standing involvement with radiation-hardened FPGAs on SLS/Orion and earlier science missions.

These partnerships highlighted $AMD is ready to capture most revenue in this space, while the rest of chip makers are still trying to figure how to make a decent CPU to lose to EPYC. 

3. High margins long term opportunity 

Space-qualified parts command significant premiums (often 5–20x or more vs. commercial equivalents) due to radiation testing, long-term reliability screening (Class B/Y, up to 15+ year missions), and low failure tolerance. A single Versal AI Edge or AI Core device in a satellite can be far more profitable than a comparable commercial chip.

SpaceX Starlink is the standout example. The constellation already has thousands of satellites in orbit, with ongoing refreshes and expansions (V2 Mini, future V3 generations). AMD's Versal AI Core adaptive SoCs are used in recent generations for high-performance onboard processing, networking, and potential AI tasks. Even if the per-satellite chip content is modest (a few devices), the scale of Starlink deployments combined with high refresh rates can drive tens of billions in annual revenue for AMD long term. Starlink itself generated ~USD 11–12 billion in revenue in 2025 and is projected to grow sharply, creating recurring demand

Companies like SpaceX and Blue Origin and $NVDA backed Starbloud are positioning to leverage near-continuous solar power, radiative cooling in vacuum (no air conditioning needed), and reduced terrestrial constraints on land, electricity grids, and water usage. 
And yes $NVDA is copying $AMD whenever it is possible, Chiplets, Quantum, CPUs, and Space-grade chips. 

Conclusion: 
While the core radiation-hardened electronics and space-grade FPGA markets remain relatively modest in absolute size with a massive long term TAM  today, AMD stands exceptionally well-positioned to capture high-margin, strategic value in the broader “AI in space” opportunity, including the long-term potential of orbital data centers.Through its Xilinx acquisition, AMD has transformed a legacy strength in radiation-tolerant FPGAs into a modern, AI-capable platform with the Versal adaptive SoC family (AI Edge and AI Core variants). These devices deliver the heterogeneous compute  programmable logic, dedicated AI engines, and Arm cores  needed for real-time inference, autonomy, data reduction, and networking under the extreme constraints of space. This capability is already flight-proven on NASA missions (Mars rovers, Artemis, OSIRIS-REx) and gaining commercial traction through high-profile partnerships.

Dr. Lisa Su has been deliberately engineering AMD to excel in this domain as part of a broader “AI everywhere” vision. At CES 2026 and in recent keynotes, she has repeatedly highlighted space exploration as a critical frontier for AMD’s adaptive computing, showcasing Blue Origin’s use of Versal technology and emphasizing real-time, reliable compute in the harshest environments from Mars rovers to future lunar permanence and beyond. By integrating Xilinx’s space-grade expertise with AMD’s Instinct GPUs, EPYC processors, and open ROCm software stack, Dr. Su is building a true compute continuum: from terrestrial data centers and edge AI down to the ultimate edge  orbit and deep space.

This strategic integration positions AMD not just to participate, but to lead in the transition toward greater spacecraft autonomy and, longer term, distributed orbital AI infrastructure. While in-orbit data centers are still early-stage and face significant technical and economic hurdles, the underlying demand for efficient, radiation-tolerant AI acceleration at the space edge aligns perfectly with AMD’s strengths. As mega-constellations scale, lunar/Mars programs mature, and concepts like Blue Origin’s Project Sunrise or SpaceX orbital compute ambitions advance, these high-margin, long-cycle opportunities could contribute meaningfully to AMD’s embedded and adaptive computing business.

kept.text:
$AMD| CPU:GPU 3-5:1 on Memory Landscape 🧵
@Samsung $MU @SKhynix 

The AI computing paradigm is undergoing a structural change. Traditional GPU-centric training workloads, which favored low CPU:GPU ratios (roughly 1 CPU to 4–8 GPUs), are giving way to inference heavy and agentic AI systems that demand far more CPU participation often shifting toward 3:1 to 5:1 or, in orchestration-intensive setups, effectively much higher CPU emphasis relative to GPUs. 

This evolution, accelerated by the move from static large language models to dynamic, multi-step agents (planning, tool-calling, retrieval, reflection, and reinforcement learning), is reshaping not just compute allocation but the entire supporting memory ecosystem from HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) dominance on GPUs to greater reliance on high-capacity DDR5/LPDDR5 variants on the CPU side, alongside hybrid designs.

AMD Suppliers:

~Samsung is main supplier: Strategic MOU for HBM4 supply to MI455X (and MI400 family) accelerators + DDR5 for EPYC Venice processors. This ensures prioritized allocation amid shortages and explores broader foundry collaboration

~Other suppliers (SK Hynix, Micron): Standard for DDR5 and additional HBM. AMD’s open ecosystem allows flexibility vs. more closed platforms.

HBM on GPUs: 
Stacked DRAM (HBM2e → HBM3 → HBM3E) delivered extreme bandwidth ( ~3+ TB/s on H100, scaling higher with HBM4). This fed compute units efficiently but offered limited capacity (typically 40–288 GB per GPU/stack) and high cost/power. Essential for minimizing data movement stalls in bandwidth-hungry training.

DDR5 on CPUs: 
Standard server memory provided high capacity (hundreds of GB to TB per node across multiple channels, ~300–800+ GB/s aggregate) at lower cost/GB. Adequate for host tasks but orders of magnitude slower in bandwidth than HBM

The $AMD custom chip for bot  is interesting with EPYC Venice and HBM4. Read the link below to understand more. For Microsoft Azure, custom AMD EPYC CPUs with High Bandwidth Memoryoffer a massive performance advantage over standard DDR5-based systems, primarily by overcoming memory bandwidth bottlenecks in AI

More CPU sockets/channels drive demand for high-capacity, cost-effective memory. DDR5 excels here for orchestration, large KV caches (which explode with agentic long contexts, 100k+ tokens requiring tens of GB), RAG databases, data pipelines, and CPU-only or hybrid inference paths.

=>Better handles memory-bound agentic tasks (e.g., tool execution, state management) without over-relying on scarce HBM. Enables larger system memory pools for disaggregated or pooled architectures 

AMD and Dr. Lisa Su have moved decisively to secure this future. Through strategic MOUs most notably with Samsung for prioritized HBM4 supply to the MI400-series accelerators and DDR5 for EPYC Venice CPUs AMD has locked in long-term memory access that extends visibility well into 2028–2030. This forward-looking supply chain strategy mitigates the wafer-allocation risks plaguing the industry, ensures balanced performance across EPYC + Instinct platforms (including the Helios rack-scale architecture), and positions AMD to capture share in an expanding CPU TAM projected for strong double-digit growth. By combining high-channel DDR5 support, secured premium memory, and an open ROCm ecosystem, AMD delivers flexible, cost-effective solutions for hyperscalers and enterprises building agentic AI factories. 

Overall, the memory ecosystem is evolving from HBM-centric scarcity to a diversified, system-optimized portfolio. Training remains bandwidth-hungry on HBM, while inference and agentic workloads thrive on CPU-driven capacity and coherence. Dr. Su securing multi-year memory pipelines, the industry is better equipped to scale efficiently, control costs, and deliver reliable performance through 2030 and beyond. The “CPU renaissance” is not a temporary correction, it is the foundation for the next decade of practical, production-grade AI.

But first, I want to clarify @AMD Forward PEG Ratio, and why UBS Upgraded AMD PT to $455 today, which is still $550 discount to where it should be, In my opinion. That is why you are seeing institutions buying the dip RIGHT NOW!

The lowest end of FY2026 projection:

The question all should ask, why most AMD analysts are not giving AMD a 1 Fwd PEG Ratio? 

AMD's EPYC Venice (6th-gen, Zen 6 launch in H2 2026) is positioned to meaningfully reduce the effective cost per token for Agentic AI workloads compared to today's Turin (5th-gen, Zen 5), with projected 45-55%+ TCO improvements at scale when it launches in H2 2026. It builds directly on Turin's already strong foundation for CPU-orchestrated inference, amplifying advantages in thread density, memory bandwidth, and efficiency for the sequential, control-flow-heavy nature of autonomous agents. 

1. Why Agentic AI changes the Tokenomics

Traditional LLM inference is GPU-bound for parallel matrix math. Agentic AI involves persistent, multi-step reasoning, tool calling, planning, routing, state/memory management, and orchestration workloads that are token-heavy and more sequential. CPUs excel as the "orchestrator," directing GPUs while handling logic loops that GPUs manage inefficiently. Hyperscalers already lean on EPYC for cost-effective batch and agentic inference due to high core counts and software compatibility.

The CPU:GPU Ratio is shifting from 1:4-8 to 3-5:1

Venice's dual-I/O die redesign and higher core counts directly target Agentic bottlenecks that Turin already mitigates well. 

Cores/Threads
Turin: 195 cores/ 384 threads
Venice: 256 cores/ 512 threads
=>More parallel orchestration & tool calls; fewer sockets needed for scale.

Process Node
Turin: TSMC 4nm/3nm
Venice: TSMC 2nm 
=>Better density, power efficiency, and scaling.

Memory Bandwidth
Turin: ~614 GB/s per socket (DDR5)
Venice: 1.6 TB/s (>2.5×) via more channels + advanced DIMMs
=> Critical for large agent contexts, multi-agent state, and reducing stalls.

CPU-GPU Bandwidth
Turin: Strong PCIe 5.0
Venice: ~2× (PCIe 6.0 + up to 128 lanes)
=> Tighter integration in Helios racks; keeps GPUs fed in agentic workflows.

TDP
Turin: 155-500W
Venice: 300-600W
=>Balanced for high-density AI nodes.

Rack Integration
Turin: Decent in existing servers
Venice: Helios Rack or Modified Rack for EPYC Dense to lower TCO even more

Most CPUs can't even compete with Turin(link CPU Comp below), and all will be behind to EPYC Venice. More tokens (and more complex agentic workflows) per dollar of hardware, per watt, and per rack. Cloud providers and self-hosters can offer lower per-token pricing or run far more agents profitably. AMD explicitly calls this out in their Agentic AI positioning, Venice powers the next wave of CPU-centric AI clusters

2. How to quantify the Cost per Token Reduction

Cost per million tokens =[(Capex amortized per year ÷ Tokens processed per year) + (Power cost per year ÷ Tokens processed per year)]

In a Turin dual-socket system

10,000 agentic tokens/sec, 900W average, $40k capex (3-yr amort = $13k/yr), assuming electric cost =$0.10/kWh.

Annual tokens (at 70% util, 24/7): ~2.2×10¹¹ tokens.

Capex/token + power/token = ~$0.0008–$0.0015/M tokens

At Rack scale optimization and 1.7× perf + >2× bandwidth + 33% density translates to 45-55% lower effective cost per token in Agentic scenarios:
This could send cost down to 
 $0.0003–$0.0005/M tokens range at hyperscale for optimized Agentic flows

This is how much it actually costs to generate or process each token (input + output + reasoning tokens) in real deployments. Especially critical for Agentic AI, where agents are "token hogs" ,  they burn far more tokens than simple chat because of multi-step reasoning, tool calls, self-correction loops, memory management, and orchestration. One agentic workflow can easily consume 5–30× more internal tokens than a single user prompt.

Conclusion:

Under Dr. Lisa Su’s leadership, AMD has been methodically engineered for exactly this moment: the J-curve inflection point where EPYC delivers the industry’s lowest tokenomics for Agentic AI customers. What began as a decade-long transformation, chiplet architecture, open ROCm ecosystem, relentless focus on heterogeneous compute, and a laser-like bet on balanced CPU+GPU systems has now converged into a structural tailwind that positions AMD as the clearest and biggest winner in the severe, multi-year CPU shortage defining the Agentic AI era.

Dr. Su has repeatedly highlighted what the market is only now fully appreciating: inference has overtaken training, and Agentic AI (with its relentless orchestration, tool-calling loops, multi-agent coordination, and persistent memory demands) has turned CPUs from supporting players into the new bottleneck. The result is a dramatic shift in data-center architecture, CPU:GPU ratios moving from 1:4–8 toward 3-5:1 or higher, driving “far exceeded expectations” demand that Lisa Su herself described as “the CPU side going like gangbusters.” Both AMD and Intel are supply-constrained, with server CPU lead times stretching to six months and prices rising 10–20%+ in recent quarters. This shortage is unlikely to resolve for years, as hyperscalers and enterprises race to deploy fleets of always-on autonomous agents that consume 5–50× more tokens per workflow than simple prompts.

Into this environment steps EPYC Venice (H2 2026, Zen 6 on TSMC 2nm). Building directly on Turin’s already-dominant foundation, Venice delivers up to 256 cores, >2.5× memory bandwidth (1.6 TB/s), PCIe 6.0 doubling of CPU-GPU interconnect, and a projected 1.7× generational performance/efficiency gain. Paired with Helios rack-scale platforms (MI455X GPUs + Venice CPUs), it is purpose-built to minimize orchestration stalls, maximize GPU utilization, and drive the lowest effective cost per million tokens to targeting the $0.0003–$0.0005 range in optimized, high-utilization deployments. Customers get dramatically better tokens per dollar, per watt, and per rack precisely when they need it most.

The economics are self-reinforcing. Persistent supply tightness creates durable pricing power and ASP uplift for EPYC, even as AMD continues to gain share toward its >50%-60% server CPU revenue target. Combined with high-margin data-center growth, this translates directly into margin expansion and outsized earnings power through 2030 and beyond.

Dr. Su didn’t just navigate AMD through the AI boom, she architected it to own the orchestration layer of the Agentic era. As the CPU Supercycle(we are 2yr in of 10yr) intensifies and tokenomics become the decisive competitive metric, EPYC Venice stands as the clearest path to the lowest-cost, highest-scale intelligence. AMD is no longer chasing the AI wave; it is the wave. The J-curve has arrived, and the margin expansion that follows will be profound.

quoted_tweet.text:
$TSM Advanced packaging capacity is maxed out.

$INTC Intel's advanced packaging business is gaining interest as an alternative to TSMC especially in the AI chip segment.

Google and Amazon are in active discussions with Intel for advanced packaging services for their custom ASICs with potential adoption of Intel's EMIB or next generation EMIB T technology for Google's TPUs and Amazon's Trainium chips.

Customer commitments for these packaging deals are expected in the second half of 2026 with possible updates at Intel's earnings call on April 23.

Intel CFO David Zinsner noted that customers are ready to commit and even prepay with potential deals reaching billions of dollars describing advanced packaging as now the more interesting and higher margin part of the foundry business with gross margins around forty percent.

The interest stems largely from TSMC's constrained advanced packaging capacity which remains heavily concentrated in Taiwan creating supply security concerns for hyperscalers.

Intel is actively expanding its packaging capacity including a new Malaysia complex set to become operational in 2026 for assembly and testing and its New Mexico fabs already in mass production of advanced 3D packaging since early 2024.

EMIB T the upgraded version of Intel's embedded multi die interconnect bridge technology adds through silicon vias for better power delivery and signal integrity and is designed to support very large AI packages.

TSMC is responding by building its own advanced packaging facility in the United States with construction starting in the second quarter of 2026 and operations targeted for late 2027 to 2028.

This development highlights growing momentum in Intel's foundry turnaround as it leverages packaging strengths to attract major AI customers amid tight industry capacity.

tweet.text:
$AMD Verse $INTC: The CPUs and Foundries 

In one of my last posts, I argued the AMD should be doing better than Intel YTD as I believe AMD makes a much better CPU chip for agentic AI than $INTC.

1) AMD CPUs offer higher core/thread density.

2) AMD's EPYC shows 1.1–1.9x gains over comparable Intel Xeon in relevant workloads

3) Market momentum: Surging demand for AMD's EPYC in agentic AI (hyperscalers, enterprises) show customers prefer AMD as the company continues to gain market share in CPUs

4) AMD's chips are significantly more energy efficient, and this matters a ton when you run them at scale at full capacity.

Now many of my commenters said it was all about the Foundry. However, I do not believe having $TSM make the CPUs for AMD is a weakness, rather a strength. 

TSM has an enormously profitable foundry business and is building hundreds of billions worth of manufacturing capacity in the United States. Meanwhile, Intel's foundry business loses tens of billions, showing it is deeply uncompetitive on a world stage. 

Commentors though Intel having their own foundry business would mean they would be able to ramp up output much faster than AMD, but I believe it actually means the opposite, it could hamstring their output and allow AMD to steal even more market share.

The European Union’s planned Chips Act II would allow the European Commission to invest directly in semiconductor manufacturing, expanding beyond its previous role of funding research and approving state aid, according to draft details.
